topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive, irritating buzz, mirroring the static in my own head. I stared at the screen, the highlighted passage mocking me with its flawless structure. Every word felt like a foreign object, alien and sharp. My hands trembled slightly as I tried to type a defense, but the keys felt heavy, unresponsive. A dull ache settled behind my eyes, a constant pressure that made focusing on the assignment feel like scaling a sheer cliff face. When the professor finally called my name, the sound seemed amplified, each syllable a hammer blow against my already fragile composure. I could only manage a shallow nod, my gaze fixed on the worn spine of the textbook, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

The accusation hung in the air like thick, suffocating smoke. I walked across the quad, the autumn chill seeping through my thin jacket and settling deep in my bones. Each step was laborious, as if wading through invisible mud. My shoulders were hunched so tightly they ached, pulling my neck into an uncomfortable angle. I kept my eyes down, focusing intently on the cracked pavement, desperate to avoid meeting anyone’s gaze. Inside, a hollow echo resonated—a profound emptiness where enthusiasm used to reside. When the committee members started speaking, their voices sounded distant, muffled, like they were broadcasting from another planet. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action feel monumental.

[story 3]

The silence in my cramped dorm room was deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs. I sat on the edge of my bed, the textbook lying open, the damning evidence starkly visible. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged thing that caught in my chest. I didn't move to gather my notes; the physical effort of reaching for anything felt insurmountable. A wave of profound weariness washed over me, heavy and paralyzing. When the accusation was finally voiced, the sound didn't register as anger or even shock; it just registered as a final, definitive closing of a door. I just stared at the ceiling, feeling utterly unmoored, as if the very ground beneath me had dissolved.
